abstract: A growing body of research debates whether Americans who express a mix of liberal and conservative policy views are genuinely cross-pressured or essentially centrist. In a high-profile study, Fowler et al. (2023) introduce a mixture model to argue that most of these voters are "Downsian" ideologues whose views are well-summarized by a single dimension, implying that those with moderate estimated scores hold "genuinely centrist" positions. This article demonstrates that the method employed by Fowler et al. systematically overstates the prevalence of ideological consistency. Because the model’s "Conversian" category is defined as a single, homogenous cluster, it fails to accommodate the diversity of non-ideological belief systems described in classical public opinion literature. Using simulations and re-analysis of the original data, we show that the model frequently misclassifies truly cross-pressured and non-ideological voters as moderate ideologues simply because a line is more geometrically flexible than a point. Consequently, the conclusion that supposedly moderate voters support centrist policies remains unsupported. We suggest that distinguishing between stable idiosyncratic views and true centrism requires richer ordinal and panel data rather than binary cross-sectional surveys.
